title: Lesson Quiz: Financial Choices and Consequences
description: Assessment of the concept that Understanding trade-offs, opportunity cost, and decision outcomes.

questions.prompt: What is a trade-off in personal finance?

choices.text: Giving up one option to choose another option
choices.feedback: Correct. A trade-off happens when choosing one option means not choosing another option.

choices.text: Receiving every option at the same time
choices.feedback: A trade-off exists because resources are limited and all options cannot usually be chosen.
A trade-off involves comparison, not ignoring choices.
A trade-off happens during decision-making, not by avoiding decisions.

questions.prompt: Opportunity cost is the value of the next best option not chosen.

choices.feedback: Opportunity cost does refer to the next best alternative that was not selected.
Correct. Opportunity cost explains what is given up when one choice is made.

questions.prompt: A student spends $20 on snacks instead of saving it for a required fee. What is the opportunity cost?

choices.feedback: The opportunity cost is the next best option given up, not the item chosen.

choices.text: The $20 that could have gone toward the required fee
choices.feedback: This idea is related, but it does not identify the opportunity cost as clearly as the correct answer.

choices.text: The lost progress toward paying the required fee
choices.feedback: Correct. The opportunity cost is the progress that could have been made toward the required fee.
That is not the next best option being given up in this decision.

questions.prompt: Why should a person compare short-term and long-term consequences before making a financial choice?

choices.feedback: Strong financial decisions should consider both short-term and long-term effects.

choices.text: Because consequences can affect both current money and future goals
choices.feedback: This is a strong idea, but the correct answer in this set more clearly frames the decision process around comparing both time horizons.

choices.text: Because future effects never change the value of a decision
choices.feedback: Future effects can strongly affect whether a decision is wise.

choices.text: Because one decision can create immediate effects now and larger effects later
choices.feedback: Correct. Comparing both time frames helps a person make a more informed financial choice.

questions.prompt: A stronger financial choice is usually supported by evidence instead of impulse.

choices.feedback: Correct. Evidence-based decisions are usually stronger than impulse choices.
Strong financial decisions usually involve comparison and reasoning, not impulse.
